title: Pseudorandom Dynamics in the SYK Model and Cryptographic Censorship in JT Gravity
abstract: We argue that the SYK model provides a conditional realization of Cryptographic Censorship in JT gravity. By using the Weingarten calculus and random matrix universality, we prove that the SYK disorder ensemble is an approximate unitary $k$-design for all $k=\poly(N)$, with deviation controlled by the spectral form factor. We then formulate the planted-SYK hardness conjecture and provide evidence from spectral universality and the low-degree polynomial framework. Under this conjecture, the approximate design becomes a gravitationally pseudorandom unitary. Together with the efficient causal wedge reconstruction in JT gravity, this leads to the conclusion that typical states in the SYK microcanonical window must have event horizons in their bulk duals, with the horizonless fraction doubly exponentially small. We further identify the regularized geodesic length of the maximal interior slice as the explicit distinguishing operator. Its prediction gap grows linearly with time due to the stretching of the black hole interior, linking Cryptographic Censorship to the complexity equals volume conjecture.
summary: This paper demonstrates that the SYK model functions as a pseudorandom unitary that hides information from efficient observers, effectively realizing 'Cryptographic Censorship' in JT gravity. It shows that the existence of an event horizon is necessary to prevent an adversary from reconstructing the black hole interior. The work connects the growth of black hole complexity to the limits of quantum information reconstruction.
main_problem: Determining whether the SYK model acts as a gravitationally pseudorandom unitary (GPRU) and whether this pseudorandomness enforces the existence of event horizons in its JT gravity dual (Cryptographic Censorship).
main_result: The paper proves the SYK disorder ensemble is an approximate unitary k-design and, under the planted-SYK hardness conjecture, a GPRU, implying that typical states must have event horizons to prevent efficient reconstruction of the black hole interior.
method: The author employs Weingarten calculus, random matrix theory, and complexity-theoretic frameworks like shadow tomography and the low-degree polynomial framework to analyze the SYK ensemble.
model_or_system: The SYK model, consisting of N Majorana fermions with all-to-all q-body random interactions, and its holographic dual, JT gravity (2D dilaton gravity).
key_observables: Spectral Form Factor (SFF), k-th frame potential, regularized geodesic length of the maximal interior slice, and prediction fidelity.
important_parameters: Number of fermions (N), scrambling time (T_scr), Heisenberg time (T_H), and the security parameter (kappa).
main_assumptions: The 'planted-SYK hardness conjecture' (computational indistinguishability from Haar) and random matrix universality.
figures_summary: Not specified in the provided notes.
paper_structure: The paper establishes the statistical properties of the SYK ensemble as a k-design, introduces the planted-SYK hardness conjecture to define GPRU status, proves the Cryptographic Censorship theorem via reconstruction limits, and links the results to the complexity equals volume conjecture.
why_interesting: It provides a deep link between computational complexity (pseudorandomness and hardness) and fundamental gravitational phenomena (event horizons and black hole interiors), which is highly relevant to researchers studying the intersection of quantum information and many-body dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Numerical verification of the frame potential analysis for SYK4 with N = 8, 10, 12 (L = 16, 32, 64), averaged over 100–300 disorder realizations. (a) The first frame potential F(1)(T) computed directly from cross-realization averages of |tr(U † 1U2)|2 (solid), compared with the analytic formula (1.1) evaluated from the disorder-averaged SFF (dashed). (b) The deviation F(1)(T) −1 as a function of T/TH, showing the approach to the τ 2 = (T/TH)2 scaling predicted by (3.9) (dashed gray line). (c) The normalized spectral form factor SFF(T)/L, exhibiting the universal slope–dip– ramp–plateau structure. Solid lines show the connected SFF; dotted lines show the disconnected part.
